current_health_status.dosage_1: 2 puffs (90 mcg/puff) as needed for wheezing or shortness of breath
current_health_status.dosage_2: 10 mg tablet once daily by mouth
current_health_status.dosage_3: 1 tablet by mouth once daily with breakfast
current_health_status.times_given_1: As needed, typically before strenuous exercise or during allergy season, not more than every 4–6 hours
current_health_status.times_given_2: Once daily in the evening around 8:00 PM
current_health_status.times_given_3: Once daily with breakfast around 7:00 AM

medical_history___musculoskeletal_neurol.allergies: Seasonal environmental allergies (pollen, grass, and ragweed) causing sneezing, itchy/watery eyes, and occasional mild wheezing during high pollen counts. No known drug allergies. No history of anaphylaxis. Uses over-the-counter antihistamines (cetirizine) and albuterol inhaler as needed during peak allergy seasons (spring and early fall).

mental_health_history_past_year.if_yes_how_often_and_for_what_reason:
I met with a licensed mental health therapist once every two weeks (about two sessions per month) from late summer last year through early spring of this year to work on stress management, motivation, and adjusting to changes at home and school. The main reasons were:

1) Falling behind in school and feeling overwhelmed: I was struggling with staying focused on assignments, keeping up with homework, and balancing school with helping out more at home. This led to a lot of stress and feeling like I was constantly behind.

2) Family stress related to my parents separating: My parents went through a separation about a year and a half ago, and although things are calmer now, I had a lot of unresolved feelings about moving between households, changes in rules, and feeling responsible for helping my younger sister adjust.

3) Low motivation and mild anxiety: I was not diagnosed with major depression or any serious mental illness, but I did have periods where I felt unmotivated, worried about my future, and uncertain about what direction I wanted to go after high school. This sometimes showed up as procrastination, staying up too late on my phone, and skipping assignments.

In therapy we talked about specific examples, like times when I would put off big projects until the last minute and then panic, or when I would get into arguments with my mom about curfew or chores. The therapist helped me learn practical tools such as making weekly schedules, breaking down big tasks into smaller steps, using breathing exercises when I felt anxious, and improving communication with my parents by being more direct about what I needed instead of just shutting down. We also discussed my interest in more structured programs (like the Youth ChalleNGe Program) as a way to reset my habits, build discipline, and get on track to finish school and plan for either college or a trade. Overall, counseling was helpful and I have not needed any emergency mental health care, hospitalization, or psychiatric medications.

additional_medical_history.additional_medical_history_within_the_pa:
Overall, I consider my health to be good, with the main ongoing issue being mild intermittent asthma that is triggered mostly by seasonal allergies and intense exercise. I have never been hospitalized for asthma, never needed a breathing tube, and have not had any emergency room visits for breathing problems in the past 12 months. The last time I needed urgent care for asthma was about two years ago when I had a bad upper respiratory infection. Since then, my symptoms have been well controlled with an albuterol inhaler used as needed and by taking cetirizine during high-pollen seasons.

In the past year, the most significant health-related events were:

1) Mild asthma flare-ups during spring allergy season: Around March and April, I noticed more frequent shortness of breath when running outside for PE and during pickup basketball games. I used my albuterol inhaler before intense activity and sometimes afterward if I felt tightness in my chest. I talked with my primary care doctor, Dr. Laura Collins, during a routine visit on 2025-04-10. She listened to my lungs, confirmed there was some mild wheezing during forced exhalation, and advised me to continue using the inhaler before exercise and to stay consistent with my allergy medication. She did not feel that I needed a daily steroid inhaler at this time because my symptoms are occasional, short-lived, and respond quickly to albuterol.

2) One minor sports strain: In October 2024, I lightly strained my right ankle playing basketball when I landed on another player’s foot. I did not hear a pop, and I was still able to walk. The ankle was a little swollen and sore for a few days, but it improved with rest, ice, compression, and elevation at home. I did not see a doctor for it, and I had no trouble returning to normal activities within about a week. I have had no ongoing pain, instability, or limitations from that incident.

3) Routine preventive care: I had a standard physical exam with Dr. Collins in April 2025. She checked my vision, hearing, lungs, heart, and general fitness. My blood pressure and heart rate were normal for my age, my weight was stable, and she did not identify any major concerns. She encouraged me to stay physically active, keep my asthma inhaler accessible, and maintain healthy sleep habits. I also had a routine dental cleaning and exam with Dr. McKenzie in January 2025, where they found no cavities. They did mention that I should floss more often to keep my gums healthy, but there were no infections or procedures beyond regular cleaning.

Other relevant information:
- I wear prescription glasses for nearsightedness, mostly for reading the board at school and for driving practice. I do not wear contact lenses.
- I sometimes experience mild motion sickness when riding in the back seat of a car on winding roads or if I try to read while riding. This usually feels like light nausea and goes away if I look out the window or sit in the front seat. I have never vomited from it in the past year and have not needed medication for it.
- I do not use tobacco, alcohol, or illegal drugs. I have been around secondhand smoke occasionally when visiting extended family, but no one smokes inside my primary home.

At this time, I am able to run, do push-ups, sit-ups, and participate in regular physical training without significant limitations as long as I pace myself, warm up properly, and use my inhaler before very intense exercise if I feel tightness starting. I have no history of heart disease, diabetes, seizures, or serious injuries. I understand that the Youth ChalleNGe Program is physically demanding, and I am motivated to manage my asthma responsibly (by carrying and using my inhaler as directed) so I can safely participate in all required activities.
